data.reasoningText:
Now I have a clear picture. This is a tiny project — one source file, one class — but it has a massive analyzer tax:

From `Contoso.WebApi.csproj`:
1. `Roslynator.Analyzers` 4.7.0
2. `SonarAnalyzer.CSharp` 9.16.0.82469
3. `Meziantou.Analyzer` 2.0.127

From `Directory.Build.props`:


4. `Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.NetAnalyzers` 8.0.0 (GlobalPackageReference)
5. `StyleCop.Analyzers` 1.1.118 (GlobalPackageReference)

Plus additional settings like `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild`, `GenerateDocumentationFile`, and `AnalysisLevel>latest-recommended` that pile on even more analysis rules. That's five analyzer packages running on a single-file project, so the Csc task is almost certainly getting buried under analyzer overhead. I need to generate a binlog to get concrete evidence of where the time is actually going.

I now have the complete picture. Here's the full diagnosis and recommendations.

---

# Build Performance Diagnosis: Contoso.WebApi

## 📊 Baseline Measurements

| Metric | With Analyzers | Without Analyzers | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Csc task** | **135 ms** | **69 ms** | **66 ms (49% of Csc)** |
| CoreCompile target | 136 ms | 71 ms | 65 ms |
| MSBuild project total | 430 ms | 436 ms | — |
| Wall-clock (3-run avg) | **~0.74s** | **~0.67s** | **~70 ms (9.5%)** |

## 🔍 Key Findings

### 1. Analyzer Overload — 27 DLLs loaded for 1 source file

The Csc compiler is loading **27 analyzer/generator DLLs** from these sources:

| Source | DLLs | Category |
|---|---|---|
| **Roslynator.Analyzers** | 9 | Third-party analyzer |
| **SonarAnalyzer.CSharp** | 4 (incl. Google.Protobuf!) | Third-party analyzer |
| **Meziantou.Analyzer** | 2 | Third-party analyzer |
| **Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.NetAnalyzers** (SDK-built-in) | 2 | SDK analyzer |
| **CodeStyle analyzers** (from `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild`) | 4 | SDK code-style |
| **NETCore.App.Ref generators** (runtime) | 6 | Framework source generators |
| **Logging.Generators** (from Logging.Abstractions) | 1 | Source generator |

**Each DLL must be loaded, initialized, and invoked by the Roslyn compiler** — even for a single 45-line file. The analyzer overhead is **66 ms = 49% of Csc time**.

### 2. Massive Rule Overlap Between Packages

These five analyzer packages have **significant diagnostic overlap**:
- **Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.NetAnalyzers** (CA/IDE rules) — *already built into the SDK when `AnalysisLevel` is set*
- **Roslynator.Analyzers** — re-implements many CA rules plus its own
- **SonarAnalyzer.CSharp** — re-implements many CA rules plus OWASP/security rules
- **Meziantou.Analyzer** — overlaps with CA1305, CA1848, etc. (we see duplicate warnings: MA0002/CA rules)
- **StyleCop.Analyzers** — referenced but **never loaded** (package not resolved; likely incompatible)

### 3. `Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.NetAnalyzers` GlobalPackageReference is Redundant

With `AnalysisLevel=latest-recommended` in Directory.Build.props, the **SDK already bundles the NetAnalyzers DLLs** from the SDK directory (`/usr/share/dotnet/sdk/.../analyzers/`). The explicit `GlobalPackageReference` for `Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.NetAnalyzers 8.0.0` is dead weight — the SDK version (bundled with SDK 10.0) takes precedence anyway.

### 4. `StyleCop.Analyzers 1.1.118` is a Ghost Reference

This package is referenced as a `GlobalPackageReference` but **no DLLs are loaded**. The package didn't even restore any analyzer DLLs. Version 1.1.118 is the legacy version — it ships analyzers only for older Roslyn/compiler versions. This is pure waste in restore and evaluation.

### 5. Build Breaks from Configuration Mismatch

`GenerateDocumentationFile=true` + `TreatWarningsAsErrors=true` causes **CS1591 errors** on every public member without XML comments. Combined with analyzer warnings promoted to errors (MA0002, CA1305, CA1848, CA1873, MA0011), the build **fails with 12 errors** — none of which are actual code bugs.

---

## ✅ Recommendations (Prioritized)

### 🟢 Quick Wins (immediate, no-risk changes)

#### 1. Remove `Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.NetAnalyzers` GlobalPackageReference
**Impact: eliminates redundancy, cleaner restore**
```xml
<!-- Directory.Build.props — REMOVE this line -->
<GlobalPackageReference Include="Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.NetAnalyzers" Version="8.0.0" />
```
The SDK already provides these analyzers when `AnalysisLevel` is set. This is documented by Microsoft: *"Starting in .NET 5, these analyzers are included with the .NET SDK."*

#### 2. Remove `StyleCop.Analyzers` GlobalPackageReference
**Impact: eliminates dead reference, faster restore**
```xml
<!-- Directory.Build.props — REMOVE this line -->
<GlobalPackageReference Include="StyleCop.Analyzers" Version="1.1.118" />
```
This package never loads any DLLs. If you want StyleCop, upgrade to `StyleCop.Analyzers 1.2.0-beta.556` or later, but note it further overlaps with the other analyzers.

#### 3. Suppress CS1591 properly for the project
**Impact: fixes the build break**
```xml
<!-- Contoso.WebApi.csproj — add to PropertyGroup -->
<NoWarn>$(NoWarn);CS1591</NoWarn>
```
Or remove `GenerateDocumentationFile` if XML docs aren't needed for this project.

### 🟡 Medium Effort (choose your analyzer stack)

#### 4. Consolidate to ONE third-party analyzer package
**Impact: ~30-50% Csc reduction (~20-33 ms saved per build)**

You're running **3 overlapping third-party analyzer packages**. Choose ONE based on your team's needs:

| If you need... | Keep | Remove |
|---|---|---|
| Broad .NET best practices | **Meziantou.Analyzer** (2 DLLs, fast) | Roslynator + SonarAnalyzer |
| IDE refactoring + analysis | **Roslynator** (9 DLLs, heavier) | Meziantou + SonarAnalyzer |
| Security/OWASP focus | **SonarAnalyzer** (4 DLLs + Protobuf) | Meziantou + Roslynator |

**My recommendation**: Keep **Meziantou.Analyzer** (lightest footprint: 2 DLLs) + the built-in SDK NetAnalyzers. That gives you CA/IDE rules from the SDK + strong MA rules, with only 2 extra DLLs instead of 15.

#### 5. Move analyzers to CI-only via `RunAnalyzers` condition
**Impact: ~49% Csc speedup for local dev builds**
```xml
<!-- Directory.Build.props -->
<PropertyGroup>
  <RunAnalyzers Condition="'$(ContinuousIntegrationBuild)' != 'true'">false</RunAnalyzers>
</PropertyGroup>
```
Then pass `-p:ContinuousIntegrationBuild=true` in CI. Developers get fast local builds; CI enforces all rules.

### 🔵 Larger Effort (architecture)

#### 6. Use `.editorconfig` to disable unused analyzer categories
Many loaded analyzers check hundreds of rules that may be irrelevant. A targeted `.editorconfig` disabling unneeded diagnostic IDs can reduce per-file analysis time, though this is harder to quantify without per-analyzer profiling.

---

## 📈 Expected Impact Summary

| Change | Csc Savings | Wall-Clock Savings |
|---|---|---|
| Remove NetAnalyzers GlobalPackageReference | ~5 ms | ~5 ms |
| Remove StyleCop ghost reference | ~0 ms (build), ~50 ms (restore) | ~50 ms restore |
| Consolidate to 1 third-party analyzer | **~30-40 ms** | **~30-40 ms** |
| `RunAnalyzers=false` for local dev | **~66 ms (100% of analyzer cost)** | **~70 ms** |
| **All combined (local dev)** | **~66 ms → Csc drops to ~69 ms** | **Build: ~0.67s (9% faster)** |

For this single-file project the absolute savings are modest (~70ms), but the **percentage** tells the real story: **analyzers consume 49% of compiler time to analyze 45 lines of code**. In a larger solution with the same analyzer stack, this overhead scales multiplicatively across every project — loading 27 DLLs and initializing hundreds of analysis rules per compilation unit. The recommendations above prevent that scaling problem before the codebase grows.
